info:
Nouns have no grammatical gender or number in most contexts.

Nouns do not inflect; they keep the same form regardless of their grammatical role.

Plurality can be optionally expressed using suffixes like 〜たち (-tachi) or 〜ら (-ra) for people or animate nouns, but context often makes number clear.

Japanese relies heavily on particles, which are small function words placed after nouns to indicate their grammatical role:

が (ga) marks the subject.

を (wo/o) marks the direct object.

に (ni) indicates direction, time, or indirect object.

へ (e) marks direction, similar to “toward.”

で (de) marks the location of an action or means/method.

の (no) indicates possession or noun-to-noun modification (like "'s" in English).

There are no articles (like "a" or "the"); definiteness is inferred through context or use of demonstratives like この (this), その (that).

Nouns have no grammatical gender or number in most contexts.

Nouns do not inflect; they keep the same form regardless of their grammatical role.

Plurality can be optionally expressed using suffixes like 〜たち (-tachi) or 〜ら (-ra) for people or animate nouns, but context often makes number clear.

Japanese relies heavily on particles, which are small function words placed after nouns to indicate their grammatical role:

が (ga) marks the subject.

を (wo/o) marks the direct object.

に (ni) indicates direction, time, or indirect object.

へ (e) marks direction, similar to “toward.”

で (de) marks the location of an action or means/method.

の (no) indicates possession or noun-to-noun modification (like "'s" in English).

There are no articles (like "a" or "the"); definiteness is inferred through context or use of demonstratives like この (this), その (that).

Nouns have no grammatical gender or number in most contexts.

Nouns do not inflect; they keep the same form regardless of their grammatical role.

Plurality can be optionally expressed using suffixes like 〜たち (-tachi) or 〜ら (-ra) for people or animate nouns, but context often makes number clear.

Japanese relies heavily on particles, which are small function words placed after nouns to indicate their grammatical role:

が (ga) marks the subject.

を (wo/o) marks the direct object.

に (ni) indicates direction, time, or indirect object.

へ (e) marks direction, similar to “toward.”

で (de) marks the location of an action or means/method.

の (no) indicates possession or noun-to-noun modification (like "'s" in English).

There are no articles (like "a" or "the"); definiteness is inferred through context or use of demonstratives like この (this), その (that).
